messages.say:
Understood: working alongside your Cursor system.
Given your request for an additional productivity tool along the lines of Affine alternatives (and referencing the comparison article you linked), here’s a smart recommendation layer based on your current Bolt DIY project setup and the need for tight, customizable integration:

🧠 Curated Affine Alternatives for Additional Productivity (User-Focused Layer)
ToolTypeWhy it Matches YouNotesAnytypeNotion + Affine hybrid (Offline-first)Local-first data model like your project emphasis, highly modular and decentralized—similar to your layered architecture styleGreat for idea development, second brain tasks, no external dependenciesReflect NotesGraph-based second brainUltra-fast graph linking between ideas; great for project-memory overlays (similar to your cursor-memory-bank)Super clean UI, Markdown-backedTanaDynamic workspace/knowledge graphsCustomizable structure + actions/events, which fits your multi-layer agent flowsCurrently invite-only but extremely powerfulLogseqOpen-source outliner / networked thinkingMarkdown local-first, backlinks; amazing for living system maps (like your phase transition workflows)Scriptable with plugins if neededCapacitiesObject-based note-takingThink: pages are types of objects (tasks, ideas, bookmarks) — aligns naturally with the modular data flow in your Bolt systemStill evolving rapidly, very structuredSupernotesCard-based productivityHighly modular, atomic units of knowledge—perfect if you want minimalism + rapid linkingThink in "knowledge packets"

🛠 Recommendation:
Primary Fit:
🔵 Anytype (for offline-first, second-brain "backbone" syncing perfectly with your modular architecture)
Secondary (layered memory bank support):
🔵 Reflect Notes or Logseq (for deep graph-memory, perfect to extend or pre-organize your cursor-memory-bank work)
Experimental (dynamic workflows):
🔵 Tana (if you want to simulate dynamic agent behavior or cross-task automation easily)

🧩 Quick Visual Fit Map to Your Project Themes:
Bolt DIY ElementBest Matched ToolMemory Bank / Phase NarrativesReflect Notes / LogseqInteractive Workflow/DiagramsTana (dynamic linking)Documentation + Scratch BuildingAnytypeIdea Growth & PrototypingCapacities / SupernotesOffline-first Critical LayeringAnytype

Would you like me to also mock up a quick "workflow insertion" sketch, showing how one of these could elegantly plug into your current Bolt DIY setup without disrupting your flow? 🚀
(Example: "Reflect Notes to organize your cursor-memory-bank sub-modes + integration map.")
Would you like that? 🎯

🤖 Understood — working alongside your Cursor system.
You picked:


Anytype (for second brain, idea/document modularity)


Excalidraw (for diagrams and visual thinking — Figma alternative option)
And you confirmed you want integration sketches for both into your Bolt DIY ecosystem.



🧠 Smart Integration Sketches (Anytype + Excalidraw)
1. 🧩 Anytype Integration Plan
ThemeHow It Fits Bolt DIYRecommendationKnowledge StructuringMirrors your cursor-memory-bank and diagrams foldersUse for modular idea trees, custom workspace per Phase (e.g., Phase 1 Research Vault, Phase 2 System Map Vault)Offline-first SyncMatches your self-hosting, decentralized mentalityTreat Anytype Vaults as draft "memory expansions" before formal Bolt DIY commitsObject-based ModelsAligns with your Component/Workflow/Object design patternsBuild custom "Object Types" like: Research Node, Agent Function, Architecture LayerAtomic NotesLike how your bolt_diy_complete_system_bundle/ bundles isolated phase narrativesCreate each "knowledge piece" as a linked atomic objectLinking/BacklinksExpands your Interdependency Maps organicallyEnable bi-directional linking for components, agents, and processesSecurity/PrivacyData remains local, like your strong Git control focusMap Anytype local data paths for easy manual Bolt DIY exports

2. 🧩 Excalidraw Integration Plan (Figma Alternative)
ThemeHow It Fits Bolt DIYRecommendationVisual Systems ThinkingComplements /diagrams/ folder (cognitive models, user paths)Build custom Excalidraw diagrams for each system model (Progressive Architecture, Data Memory Flow)Self-hosted ReadyExcalidraw allows local file save with .excalidraw formatSave raw .excalidraw JSON files inside /diagrams/ for versioningCollaborator FriendlyEasy for quick edits, low barrier to entryUse for early-stage brainstorms before promoting diagrams to Bolt-style finalized assetsPlugin SupportExcalidraw+ or VSCode plugin can allow embedding directlyAdd a tools/excalidraw_importer.ts if you want lightweight loading of .excalidraw files dynamically in frontendLightweight SVG ExportCan export to SVGs for integration into /assets/Include exported SVG versions for Phase validation documents and visual deliverablesFlexibility vs FigmaNo bloated overhead, fast prototypingUse Excalidraw for thinking and draft visualization, leave Figma-style polish to later stages if needed

🧩 Visual Sketch: Workflow Mapping into Bolt DIY
(User Mode)                             (Bolt DIY System)
     ↓                                         ↓
Anytype:                                   /cursor-memory-bank/
- Vaults per Phase                         - Modular subfolders
- Atomic linked notes      -->             - Custom Modes
- Local-first objects                      - Memory expansions

Excalidraw:                                /diagrams/
- System maps                              - Progressive Architecture
- User journey sketches    -->             - Cognitive Interaction Model
- Flowcharts, mindmaps                     - Phase Transition Visuals

Common Points:
- Version controlled locally
- Exports to standardized formats (.excalidraw, .svg, .md)
- Supports phased narrative development


🧩 Example Setup Proposals
(Feel free to ask and I can even draft file structure upgrades for you.)
Anytype Vaults to create:


Bolt DIY Phase 0 — Discovery


Bolt DIY Phase 1 — Research


Bolt DIY Phase 2 — Planning


Bolt DIY Phase 3 — Building


Bolt DIY Phase 4 — Scaling


Excalidraw Folder Additions:


/diagrams/early_sketches/


/diagrams/user_flow_mocks/


/diagrams/system_maps/



🛠️ Optional Extensions


✨ Build a VSCode Task to auto-copy new .excalidraw files to your /diagrams/ repo folder.


✨ Add a BoltDIY diagrams-loader.ts to view/update .excalidraw files inside your interface dynamically.



Would you like me next to:


Draft folder templates for each (Anytype, Excalidraw) ready for your project?


Or show an even deeper integration flowchart where tasks begin in Anytype, draft diagrams in Excalidraw, then finalize into Bolt DIY memory structure?


🎯
Which one would you prefer first?
(Or both — can go fast if you want!) 🚀

🤖 Understood — staying in strategic mode, taking your request seriously and methodically.

🧠 Option 1: Draft Folder Templates First (Anytype + Excalidraw)
Drafting folder templates first offers immediate structural clarity for how these new tools (Anytype and Excalidraw) will plug into your existing Bolt DIY codebase. By aligning with your current /cursor-memory-bank/ and /diagrams/ architecture, templates ensure the incoming workflows have a clear "home" — promoting smooth, stress-free integration from day one. You would quickly gain a scaffold where ideas, diagrams, notes, and visual systems already have designated containers, reducing friction when you later start creating content. Especially given that Bolt DIY’s structure is heavily modular (as seen in your codebase.md​codebase), pre-building these templates preserves your modular philosophy and promotes consistent naming, clean version control, and high retrievability across projects.
Additionally, templates set a "culture" inside your system: they communicate expectations to future collaborators (or even to your future self) about where everything belongs. In your very layered environment, where different tools serve different purposes at different stages (Phase 1, Phase 2, etc.), clear containers lead to clear minds — accelerating productivity without sacrificing order.

🧠 Option 2: Draft Integration Flowchart First (Anytype → Excalidraw → Bolt DIY)
Alternatively, drafting an integration flowchart first captures the dynamic behavior and mental model behind how these new tools (Anytype, Excalidraw) will interact across different stages of your workflow. Instead of only defining where the files will live, this option defines how and when these tools are used. It helps you avoid over-engineering folder structures prematurely, especially if workflows evolve after a few weeks of actual use. A flowchart would visually map scenarios like: "Idea born in Anytype → Diagram drafted in Excalidraw → Finalized and versioned in Bolt DIY," preserving the fluidity and experimental edge of your Bolt DIY system.
More importantly, a good integration map acts as a contract between mental models and operational reality: it ensures that each tool plays to its strengths, while reducing redundancy (e.g., not duplicating knowledge across 3 places without purpose). In a project as layered and modular as yours, understanding the sequence and rationale of how tools are intended to interact could be even more foundational than static file structures. Particularly when you plan to scale Bolt DIY later with possible collaborators or agent-based extensions, dynamic flow understanding will future-proof your project far more elegantly.

🎯 Verdict: Draft Integration Flowchart First is obviously better.
Because:


Your system (Bolt DIY) thrives on dynamic modularity, adaptive evolution, and flexible mental layering.


Locking in folder templates first risks building around wrong assumptions about tool usage sequences.


A flowchart is a living map — you can adjust folder structures later once the actual behavioral flow is clear.


You are already managing multi-phase complexity; you need a flow-first, structure-second approach to remain scalable and agile.



Would you like me to immediately draft that Integration Flowchart now? 🎯
(Structured precisely around your Phase methodology + Bolt DIY architecture.)
Ready when you are! 🚀

🤖 Understood — flowing carefully into the Integration Flowchart first as strategically agreed.
Here's the first-pass draft to match your Bolt DIY system architecture, your Phase narratives, and your modular intent:

🧠 Integration Flowchart: Anytype + Excalidraw → Bolt DIY
[Idea / Requirement Arises]
        ↓
[Anytype Vault]
- Create Atomic Note
- Link to Related Notes
- Organize by Phase Vault (Phase 1 Research, etc.)
        ↓
[Idea Becomes Visualizable]
        ↓
[Excalidraw Sketch]
- Rough Draft Diagram
- Mindmap, Workflow, Flowchart
- Save .excalidraw file
- Optional: Export SVG if polished
        ↓
[Validation / Approval Checkpoint]
- Self-review
- Peer review (if future team expansion)
        ↓
[Commit to Bolt DIY]
|   → [Textual Components]
|        - Paste refined Anytype text into `/cursor-memory-bank/`
|        - Integrate into phase narratives or memory guides
|
|   → [Visual Components]
|        - Save .excalidraw JSON into `/diagrams/early_sketches/`
|        - Export SVG for `/assets/`
|
|   → [Optional Enhancements]
|        - Link Excalidraw diagrams into Narrative Docs
|        - Map Anytype Objects to JSON-compatible export for auto-import later
        ↓
[Deployment / Iteration]
- Files committed into Git
- Refine in-place as system matures
- Use Memory Bank Upgrades to periodically rationalize structure


🧩 Visual Shape (Fast Glance Version)
Anytype (Idea Capture)
    ↓
  Excalidraw (Diagram Drafting)
    ↓
Validation Gate (Review)
    ↓
Bolt DIY Systemization
  ↙         ↘
Memory Bank   Diagrams/Assets
(Text)        (Visuals)


🛠 Notes on Flow Best Practices


Don't Over-Polish in Anytype or Excalidraw early → rough is good. Bolt DIY is the refinement gate.


Version Excalidraw Sketches separately if used heavily (maybe auto-generate v1, v2, v3 filenames).


Treat Excalidraw JSON as source of truth even if exporting SVG for front-facing use.


Batch Import to Memory Bank only when concept is stable enough (no rushing phases).


Preserve Phase Linkage at every step (Phase 1 sketches go to Phase 1 docs, etc.)



🌟 Why this is powerful for you:


You stay lightweight and experimental until you know the concept is ready.


You respect modular thinking without falling into folder rigidity early.


You future-proof the Bolt DIY ecosystem to allow higher agent-driven automation later (structured phases are easy to parse programmatically).



🧩 Suggested Immediate Actions
TaskDescriptionOptional EnhancementsCreate Anytype VaultsPhase-specific: Research, Planning, Building, ScalingCustom Templates (Idea, Decision Log, etc.)Create Excalidraw "Workspace Folders"/early_sketches/, /finalized_maps/ inside /diagrams/Setup VSCode Excalidraw PluginSetup Review GateDecide: simple checklist for promotion from draft → Bolt DIYGit pre-commit hook for large diagram changes

Next:
Would you like me to build you a companion mini checklist to formalize this "flow gate" (i.e., when to promote from Anytype/Excalidraw into Bolt DIY)?
It'll be short and tactical — ready to use immediately if you want. 🎯
Want me to proceed with that? ✅

🤖 Understood — continuing the flow-first strategy you approved.
Here’s the Flow Gate Checklist:
Simple, tactical, non-bureaucratic — designed for speed but sharpness so you only promote high-quality ideas into Bolt DIY.

🧠 Flow Gate Checklist (Anytype → Excalidraw → Bolt DIY)
📚 For Anytype Notes:


 Phase Tag Applied: (Discovery, Research, Planning, Building, Scaling)


 Title is Clear: Describes the idea or decision in one strong sentence.


 Atomic: Focused on one key concept or logical cluster (no rambling multi-topics).


 Linked: Has at least one meaningful link to another note or object.


 Confidence Level > 70%: You believe the note captures a sound foundation worth building on.



🎨 For Excalidraw Diagrams:


 Has a Clear Purpose: (Mindmap? System Map? User Journey? Workflow?)


 Logical Layout: Flow is understandable without outside explanation.


 Minimal Distraction: Sketchy OK, but no confusing excess (empty boxes, misaligned arrows, unclear labels).


 Saved in Both: .excalidraw (source) + .svg (optional export for visual integration).


 Versioned if Iterated: If you redraw, save as diagram-name_v2.excalidraw, etc.



🚀 Before Promotion to Bolt DIY:


 Phase Matched: Ready content is slotted under correct Bolt DIY Phase.


 Reviewed (Solo or Team): You've sanity-checked that it's not a half-formed idea.


 Commit Reason Identified: Why am I locking this into system memory? (e.g., "Solidified Data Flow for Phase 2 Planning")


 Naming Convention Clean: Folders/files use slug-style: phase2_user_flow_map.excalidraw, phase1_discovery_notes.md, etc.



🎯 Flow-Gate Philosophy:
"If it’s still evolving rapidly, keep in Anytype/Excalidraw.
If it’s coherent enough to teach someone else, promote it into Bolt DIY."

🛠 Bonus Tip
You can embed this checklist inside:


A pinned Anytype Template (called “Bolt Promotion Gate”)


A /cursor-memory-bank/flow_gates/ folder for process tracking


A simple Notion table or even Excalidraw itself if you want visual gate tracking!



📦 Ready-to-Use Quick Card Summary
[ Phase Tag ] ✅
[ Atomic Note / Clear Diagram ] ✅
[ Links / Logical Layout ] ✅
[ Confidence >70% ] ✅
[ Saved Correctly (.excalidraw, .svg) ] ✅
[ Naming Clean ] ✅
[ Clear Commit Reason ] ✅


✅ Now you have both:


Full Integration Flowchart (mental model)


Full Flow Gate Checklist (operational gatekeeper)



Would you also like me to next propose some lightweight folder names (specifically for /diagrams/ and /cursor-memory-bank/) that match this flow so it’s even easier to organize as you promote work? 🚀
It would be super fast, and totally tuned to the flow you just set up.

🤖 Understood — stepping carefully and methodically here to make this ultra-practical for you.
Here’s a clean guide on how you use what we've discussed so far — without overcomplicating anything:

🧠 1. Capture Raw Ideas in Anytype


Whenever you have an idea (new feature, architecture design, process improvement, system narrative),
start it inside Anytype, not Bolt DIY.


Use Atomic Notes → one idea per note.


Apply a Phase Tag immediately (e.g., "Phase 1: Research").


Link related notes together inside Anytype — this forms proto-memory webs.


✅ You are using Anytype as your idea staging ground.

🎨 2. Sketch System Concepts in Excalidraw


When your idea feels ready to "map out" visually (e.g., workflows, data flows, cognitive models),
create a rough draft in Excalidraw.


Save it as a .excalidraw file (source of truth).


(Optional) Export it as an .svg for later visual embedding.


✅ You are using Excalidraw as your flexible thinking and diagram drafting space.

🛡 3. Use the Flow Gate Checklist


Before promoting any note or diagram into Bolt DIY:
Open the Flow Gate Checklist.


Ask yourself:


Is the Anytype note atomic and linked?


Is the Excalidraw diagram clear and logical?


Is this ready for the system memory or still evolving?




Only promote work that crosses the gate.


✅ You maintain high quality and modularity, no clutter inside Bolt DIY.

🛠 4. Commit into Bolt DIY System


Textual Content:
Paste refined Anytype notes into /cursor-memory-bank/, organized by Phase folders.


Visual Content:
Place .excalidraw and .svg files into /diagrams/, organized by type (e.g., /early_sketches/, /final_maps/).


✅ You are systemizing mature thoughts only, keeping your Bolt DIY ecosystem clean and scale-ready.

🗺 Summary of Your New Operational Flow
StageToolActionLocationIdea CaptureAnytypeAtomic NotesLocal VaultsVisual DraftExcalidrawSketch DiagramsLocal File (.excalidraw)ValidationChecklistFlow GateInternalPromotionBolt DIYCommit Notes + Diagrams/cursor-memory-bank/, /diagrams/

🌟 Why This Is So Powerful for You


You avoid premature over-organization (which can paralyze creative flow).


You promote only strong, reviewed, linked ideas into Bolt DIY.


You reinforce a phased, iterative memory system that mirrors how real world system design evolves.



📦 If you want, you can immediately:


Create an "Anytype Vault" with Phase folders: (Discovery, Research, Planning, etc.)


Set up /diagrams/early_sketches/ and /diagrams/final_maps/ inside Bolt DIY.


Pin the Flow Gate Checklist inside Anytype and/or your workspace.


Try one full cycle with a new idea this week — even something small — and experience the flow.

It would summarize this entire operational loop at a glance.
(Helpful if you like visual anchors while you work.)
Want me to build it? 🎯

Want me to? 🚀

Of course — here’s a clean, detailed summary of our entire journey so far, written precisely to match the tone and depth you requested:

🧩 Integration Strategy Journey Summary — (Anytype + Excalidraw → Bolt DIY)

🌟 Starting Point
You sought an additional productivity layer aligned with your modular Bolt DIY system. After reviewing Affine alternatives, you selected:


Anytype (for decentralized second-brain style idea management)


Excalidraw (for fast, flexible diagram drafting — as a Figma alternative)


You requested a serious evaluation of how best to integrate these tools without losing Bolt DIY’s system modularity, agility, or phase-based organization.

🛠 Two Strategic Paths Considered
We debated two options:


Option 1: Build folder templates immediately


Option 2: Build an integration flowchart first


After reflecting carefully on your system's nature (dynamic, evolutionary, modular), we chose Option 2 — drafting a dynamic integration flowchart first — to avoid rigid assumptions and keep Bolt DIY adaptable as real workflows develop.

🧠 Integration Flowchart Delivered
We mapped a clear flow:
[Anytype] → [Excalidraw] → [Validation Gate] → [Bolt DIY Memory System]



Anytype captures atomic notes, linked into knowledge webs by phase (Discovery, Research, Planning, Building, Scaling).


Excalidraw visualizes maturing ideas into mindmaps, system maps, or workflows.


A Flow Gate Checklist ensures only mature, linked, confident ideas are promoted into Bolt DIY.


Bolt DIY receives finalized textual memory (via /cursor-memory-bank/) and diagrams (via /diagrams/).


This respected your multi-phase narrative structure, your mental workflow, and your operational scaling goals.

🛡 Flow Gate Checklist Created
We built a tactical checklist that defines readiness for promotion:


Clear phase tagging


Atomicity of notes


Logical, minimal diagrams


Confidence threshold (>70%)


Clean slug-style naming conventions


Clear commit reasons when finalizing


This checklist acts as a mental and operational quality gate to keep your system clean, purposeful, and future-proof.

📋 Ready-to-Use Deliverables
We then packaged everything into actionable artifacts:


Bolt DIY Flowcard — A workspace-friendly printable cheat sheet summarizing your entire operational loop at a glance.


Bolt DIY Mini Digital Template — A duplicable outline you can embed in Anytype or Excalidraw, supporting fast and standardized tracking of each new idea through the phases.



🎯 Final Alignment
By the end, you now have:


A fluid capture-to-system workflow spanning Anytype, Excalidraw, and Bolt DIY.


A flow-first, gate-kept, quality-driven system that mirrors how real large-scale architectures grow sustainably.


A lightweight, elegant operating protocol that matches your original modular vision but expands it with stronger flexibility, scaling potential, and creative flow protection.



Would you also like me to append a short "Operational Best Practices" section at the bottom of your Canvas ("Anytype Bolt Mapping") based on this summary?
Something you can glance at weekly to stay razor-aligned? 🚀
(Only if you want — ready if so.)

🤖 Understood — drafting a tight, sharp Operational Best Practices section
to append cleanly to your "Anytype Bolt Mapping" Canvas.
Here it is:

🛡 Operational Best Practices for Anytype → Excalidraw → Bolt DIY

📚 Capture Phase (Anytype)


Atomic First: Break ideas into the smallest meaningful units.


Phase Tag Early: Always tag notes immediately (Discovery, Research, Planning, Building, Scaling).


Link Boldly: Build webs of related knowledge — ideas should connect or be challenged.



🎨 Visualization Phase (Excalidraw)


Draft Fast, Think Clear: Focus on conveying logical flow, not polished aesthetics.


Save in Both Formats: .excalidraw (source) always; .svg optionally for clean visuals.


Label, Not Explain: Diagrams should largely explain themselves via labels and flows.



🛡 Validation Phase (Flow Gate)


Checklist Discipline: Never skip the Flow Gate — check Confidence, Atomicity, Logical Links, Naming.


Delay Promotion: If uncertain, keep working inside Anytype/Excalidraw. Promote only when coherence crystallizes.



🛠 Promotion Phase (Bolt DIY)


Slugify Everything: Use clean, lowercased, hyphenated file names (phase2-user-flow-map.excalidraw).


Folder Respect: Diagrams in /diagrams/, notes in /cursor-memory-bank/ — maintain clear separation.


Commit With Reason: Every commit into Bolt DIY memory must have a "why now" justification.



🎯 Core Guiding Philosophy

"Draft rough. Link strong. Promote only what teaches."


✅ This Best Practices section is now ready to directly paste/append under your "Anytype Bolt Mapping" Canvas!

🧭 Reminder Motto

"Capture lightly, connect deeply, commit only when clarity demands."


🧩 Why this fits you perfectly:


Capture lightly → protects creative flow in Anytype and Excalidraw (no perfectionism early).


Connect deeply → encourages meaningful linkage across phases and systems (not isolated ideas).


Commit only when clarity demands → forces rigor before promoting into Bolt DIY memory, safeguarding system purity.



✅

At the top of your Anytype Vaults (as a page title or header block)

As a sticky note next to your working terminal or desk setup

Great — let’s walk through cleaning the defaults in Anytype and setting up your custom Phase-aligned workspace using the vault header and flow we’ve built.

🧼 Step 1: Clean the Default Anytype Setup
✅ What to remove:


Welcome to Anytype page


About Anytype page


Default Tasks object


🔧 How to remove:


Right-click or ... on each item (e.g., “Welcome to Anytype”)


Select Archive or Delete (your version may only offer “Archive” — that’s fine)


Confirm when prompted



✨ This clears your workspace so you’re not distracted by onboarding clutter.


🧱 Step 2: Create Your Phase Vault Pages
📁 Create 5 New Pages:
From the + icon (top right of "My First Space") or the “Pages” block:


Phase 0 – Discovery


Phase 1 – Research


Phase 2 – Planning


Phase 3 – Building


Phase 4 – Scaling


You can optionally group them into a "Bolt DIY" Collection later (I can help with that).

📌 Step 3: Set Up Your Anchor Page


Create a new page called:
⚓ Bolt DIY System Anchor


Paste the following template into the new page:


# 🧭 Reminder Motto
> **"Capture lightly, connect deeply, commit only when clarity demands."**

---

# 🧩 Phase Structure
- 🏁 **Discovery Phase** — Raw ideas, seeds, inspiration
- 🔍 **Research Phase** — Linked knowledge webs, technical exploration
- 🛠 **Planning Phase** — Structured drafts, early systems modeling
- 🏗 **Building Phase** — Concrete design, architecture, flows
- 🚀 **Scaling Phase** — Optimization, scaling systems, advanced integration

---

# 🛡 Flow Reminder
[Anytype (Idea Capture)] → [Excalidraw (Visual Draft)] → [Flow Gate Validation] → [Bolt DIY Commit]



Pin this page to the sidebar by starring it or adding it to your Favorites for fast access.



🗂️ Suggested Anytype Folder Flow
📁 My First Space
├── ⚓ Bolt DIY System Anchor
├── Phase 0 – Discovery
├── Phase 1 – Research
├── Phase 2 – Planning
├── Phase 3 – Building
└── Phase 4 – Scaling

Optional:


Group them under a "Collection" named Bolt DIY Phases


Use Templates to standardize new notes or pages in each Phase



✅ Done: You now have:


A clean, clutter-free workspace


A modular Phase-based structure


An operational anchor and motto pinned front and center


Seamless integration with the Bolt DIY pipeline we designed
